problem_text: BluePeak Batteries launches a new campus product. Initial demand is Qd = 111 - 2P and supply is Qs = 21 + 3P. A successful promotion shifts demand outward by 19 units at every price.
solution:
(a) Initial equilibrium solves 111 - 2P = 21 + 3P, so P = $18 and Q = 75.

(b) New demand is Qd = 130 - 2P. Solving gives P = $21.80 and Q = 86.40.

(c) The outward demand shift raises both equilibrium price and equilibrium quantity.

solution_subparts.text: Initial equilibrium solves 111 - 2P = 21 + 3P, so P = $18 and Q = 75.

solution_subparts.label: b

solution_subparts.label: c
solution_subparts.text: The outward demand shift raises both equilibrium price and equilibrium quantity.

subparts.label: b
subparts.text: Compute the new equilibrium after the demand shift.

subparts.label: c
subparts.text: Explain what happens to price and quantity.

problem_text: A regulator caps the price of Cobalt Cloud's rental permits at $22. Market demand is Qd = 142 - 4P and market supply is Qs = 10 + 2P.
solution:
(a) Set demand equal to supply: 142 - 4P = 10 + 2P, so P = $22.

(b) The ceiling is not binding because $22 is equal to the equilibrium price of $22.

(c) Because the ceiling is not binding, there is no shortage. At $22, Qd = 54 and Qs = 54.

problem_text: Crimson Kite sells a product eligible for a buyer subsidy of $8. Demand is Qd = 123 - 3Pb, where Pb is the buyer's out-of-pocket price. Supply is Qs = 13 + 3Ps, where Ps is the seller's received price.
solution:
(a) Without subsidy, Pb = Ps = P. Solving gives P = $18.33.

(b) The subsidy means Ps = Pb + 8. Set 123 - 3Pb = 13 + 3(Pb + 8). Equivalently Ps = $22.33, Pb = $14.33, and Q = 80.

(c) The subsidy drives a wedge that lowers the buyer price and raises the seller price, increasing quantity traded.

(b) New supply is Qs = -1 + 2P. Solving gives P = $33.75 and Q = 66.50.

(c) A leftward supply shift raises price and lowers quantity.

problem_text: Flux Fitness sells same-day delivery passes in a competitive city market. Demand is Qd = 155 - 3P and supply is Qs = 17 + 2P. The city is considering a seller tax of $6 per pass.
solution:
(a) Set 155 - 3P = 17 + 2P. P = $27.60 and Q = 72.20.

(b) With the tax, sellers receive Ps = Pc - 6. Set 155 - 3Pc = 17 + 2(Pc - 6). Pc = $30, Ps = $24, and Q = 65.

(c) Buyers pay $2.40 more; sellers receive $3.60 less. The larger price change is the larger burden.

problem_text: Meridian Micro sells a product eligible for a buyer subsidy of $8. Demand is Qd = 148 - 3Pb, where Pb is the buyer's out-of-pocket price. Supply is Qs = 18 + 3Ps, where Ps is the seller's received price.
solution:
(a) Without subsidy, Pb = Ps = P. Solving gives P = $21.67.

(b) The subsidy means Ps = Pb + 8. Set 148 - 3Pb = 18 + 3(Pb + 8). Equivalently Ps = $25.67, Pb = $17.67, and Q = 95.

(c) The subsidy drives a wedge that lowers the buyer price and raises the seller price, increasing quantity traded.

solution:
(a) Q = 201 - 4(25) = 101.

problem_text: When a rival raises its price from $12 to $14, Echo Forge's weekly sales rise from 908 to 970 units.
solution:
(a) Cross-price elasticity is (970 - 908)/908 divided by (14 - 12)/12 = 0.41.

(b) It is positive, so the goods are substitutes.

(c) The firm may gain customers when the rival raises price, so monitoring competitor prices is valuable.

problem_text: As average customer income rises from $83 thousand to $91 thousand, annual purchases of Flux Fitness' service rise from 406 to 449.
solution:
(a) Income elasticity is (449 - 406)/406 divided by (91 - 83)/83 = 1.10.

(b) The elasticity is positive, so it is a normal good.

(c) Since the value is above 1, it is more luxury-like.

(b) With Ps = Pb + 6, solving gives Ps = $24.50, Pb = $18.50, Q = 66.

(c) Legal receipt is about who gets the check; economic incidence is about how market prices change.

problem_text: A per-unit tax of $8 is proposed for the market in which Nebula Noodles operates. Demand is Qd = 115 - 2P and supply is Qs = 17 + 2P.
solution:
(a) Before tax, P = $24.50 and Q = 66. After tax, Pc = $28.50, Ps = $20.50, and Q = 58.

(b) DWL = 0.5 x tax x quantity reduction = 0.5 x 8 x 8 = $32.

(c) Some mutually beneficial trades no longer occur because the tax wedge raises buyer cost above seller receipt.

problem_text: The state offers buyers of Cobalt Cloud's product a subsidy of $6 per unit. Demand is Qd = 118 - 2Pb and supply is Qs = 14 + 2Ps.

(b) With Ps = Pb + 6, solving gives Ps = $29, Pb = $23, Q = 72.

(c) Legal receipt is about who gets the check; economic incidence is about how market prices change.

problem_text: The state offers buyers of Prism Produce's product a subsidy of $6 per unit. Demand is Qd = 148 - 2Pb and supply is Qs = 14 + 2Ps.
solution:
(a) No subsidy gives P = $33.50.

(b) With Ps = Pb + 6, solving gives Ps = $36.50, Pb = $30.50, Q = 87.

(c) Legal receipt is about who gets the check; economic incidence is about how market prices change.

problem_text: Imports of an input used by Quantum Kettle are capped at 49 units. Domestic demand is Qd = 169 - 3P and domestic supply is Qs = 11 + 2P.
solution:
(a) With quota imports, Qd = Qs + 49.

(b) 169 - 3P = 11 + 2P + 49, so P = $36.33.

(c) Domestic production is Qs = 11 + 2(36.33) = 83.67.

solution_subparts.label: a
solution_subparts.text: With quota imports, Qd = Qs + 49.

solution_subparts.label: c
solution_subparts.text: Domestic production is Qs = 11 + 2(36.33) = 83.67.

subparts.label: a
subparts.text: Write the market-clearing condition with the import quota.

subparts.label: c
subparts.text: Find domestic production at that price.

problem_text: A per-unit tax of $8 is proposed for the market in which Sapphire Solar operates. Demand is Qd = 160 - 2P and supply is Qs = 14 + 2P.
solution:
(a) Before tax, P = $36.50 and Q = 87. After tax, Pc = $40.50, Ps = $32.50, and Q = 79.

(b) DWL = 0.5 x tax x quantity reduction = 0.5 x 8 x 8 = $32.

(c) Some mutually beneficial trades no longer occur because the tax wedge raises buyer cost above seller receipt.

subparts.label: a
subparts.text: Find quantity before and after the tax.

problem_text: Nebula Noodles' home country is considering opening to free trade. Domestic demand is Qd = 152 - 3P, domestic supply is Qs = 12 + 2P, and the world price is $44.
solution:
(a) At $44, Qd = 20 and Qs = 100. Since supply exceeds demand, the country exports.

(b) Exports are Qs - Qd = 80.

(c) Domestic producers benefit from the higher selling opportunity; domestic consumers face the world price.

problem_text: A lobby asks for a tariff on the imported component used by Polaris Bikes. Demand is Qd = 154 - 3P and supply is Qs = 2P. The world price is $20 and the proposed tariff is $9.
solution:
(a) Consumers lose from the price increase to $29; domestic producers gain because their output rises from 40 to 58.

(b) Revenue is $9 x imports 9 = $81.

(c) The tariff creates production and consumption distortions: some efficient imports are replaced or not consumed.

problem_text: Nova Basket produces output with q = sqrt(LK). It must produce q = 105. Labor costs $21 and capital costs $6.
solution:
(a) MPL/MPK = K/L must equal w/r = 21/6. Thus K/L = 3.50.

(b) Using LK = q^2 and K/L = w/r gives L = 56.12 and K = 196.44.

(c) Cost is wL + rK = $2357.24.

problem_text: Polaris Bikes' long-run cost is C(q) = 66 + 10q + q^2.
solution:
(a) ATC = 66/q + 10 + q.

(b) Minimize 66/q + q. The minimum occurs at q = sqrt(66) = 8.12.

(c) Minimum ATC is 10 + 2sqrt(66) = $26.25.

problem_text: Prism Produce observes total cost of $524 at 53 units and $740 at 83 units over the relevant range.
solution:
(a) Marginal cost is approximately Delta C / Delta q = (740 - 524)/(83 - 53) = $7.20.

(b) Yes, if price exceeds marginal cost, expanding output raises profit locally.

(c) This is an average slope over a range, not necessarily the exact marginal cost at every quantity.

problem_text: Quantum Kettle doubles both labor and capital in a production process described by q = 2L^0.5K^0.5.
solution:
(a) Doubling both inputs multiplies output by 2^0.5 x 2^0.5 = 2, so output exactly doubles.

(b) The technology has constant returns to scale.

(c) With constant returns, scaling the plant up does not by itself lower or raise unit input needs.

(a) MPL/MPK = K/L must equal w/r = 21/8. Thus K/L = 2.62.

(b) Using LK = q^2 and K/L = w/r gives L = 95.67 and K = 251.13.

(c) Cost is wL + rK = $4018.06.

solution_subparts.text: MPL/MPK = K/L must equal w/r = 21/8. Thus K/L = 2.62.

subparts.text: Write the tangency condition for cost minimization.

problem_text: BluePeak Batteries' long-run cost is C(q) = 76 + 10q + q^2.
solution:
(a) ATC = 76/q + 10 + q.

(b) Minimize 76/q + q. The minimum occurs at q = sqrt(76) = 8.72.

(c) Minimum ATC is 10 + 2sqrt(76) = $27.44.

solution_subparts.label: b
solution_subparts.text: Minimize 76/q + q. The minimum occurs at q = sqrt(76) = 8.72.

solution_subparts.label: c
solution_subparts.text: Minimum ATC is 10 + 2sqrt(76) = $27.44.

subparts.label: b
subparts.text: Find the output that minimizes average total cost.

problem_text: Cobalt Cloud observes total cost of $604 at 63 units and $840 at 93 units over the relevant range.
solution:
(a) Marginal cost is approximately Delta C / Delta q = (840 - 604)/(93 - 63) = $7.87.

(b) Yes, if price exceeds marginal cost, expanding output raises profit locally.

(c) This is an average slope over a range, not necessarily the exact marginal cost at every quantity.

problem_text: Crimson Kite doubles both labor and capital in a production process described by q = 3L^0.5K^0.5.
solution:
(a) Doubling both inputs multiplies output by 2^0.5 x 2^0.5 = 2, so output exactly doubles.

(b) The technology has constant returns to scale.

(c) With constant returns, scaling the plant up does not by itself lower or raise unit input needs.

subparts.label: c

problem_text: Quantum Kettle doubles both labor and capital in a production process described by q = 3L^0.5K^0.5.
solution:
(a) Doubling both inputs multiplies output by 2^0.5 x 2^0.5 = 2, so output exactly doubles.

(b) The technology has constant returns to scale.

(c) With constant returns, scaling the plant up does not by itself lower or raise unit input needs.

problem_text: Summit Seltzer produces output with q = sqrt(LK). It must produce q = 330. Labor costs $21 and capital costs $7.
solution:
(a) MPL/MPK = K/L must equal w/r = 21/7. Thus K/L = 3.

(b) Using LK = q^2 and K/L = w/r gives L = 190.53 and K = 571.58.

(c) Cost is wL + rK = $8002.07.

problem_text: Vertex Coffee's long-run cost is C(q) = 111 + 10q + q^2.

(a) ATC = 111/q + 10 + q.

(b) Minimize 111/q + q. The minimum occurs at q = sqrt(111) = 10.54.

(c) Minimum ATC is 10 + 2sqrt(111) = $31.07.

problem_text: Vanta Vacuum observes total cost of $884 at 98 units and $1190 at 128 units over the relevant range.
solution:
(a) Marginal cost is approximately Delta C / Delta q = (1190 - 884)/(128 - 98) = $10.20.

(b) Yes, if price exceeds marginal cost, expanding output raises profit locally.

(c) This is an average slope over a range, not necessarily the exact marginal cost at every quantity.

(c) Fixed cost is sunk in the short run and does not determine the shutdown price.

problem_text: BluePeak Batteries is one of 20 identical competitive firms. Each firm has MC = 8 + 2q. Market demand is Qd = 1050 - 5P.

(c) Each firm produces q = (75.33 - 8)/2 = 33.67.

problem_text: In a long-run competitive industry, firms like Cobalt Cloud have cost C(q) = 131 + 11q + q^2.

(b) Minimum ATC occurs at q = sqrt(131) = 11.45.

(c) Positive profits attract entry, shifting industry supply outward and pushing price down.

problem_text: Crimson Kite has cost C(q) = 112 + 12q + q^2. The market price has fallen to $11.

(a) AVC = 12 + q, whose minimum is $12 as q approaches zero.

(b) Since price $11 is below the shutdown price $12, the firm should shut down.

(c) Fixed cost is sunk in the short run and does not determine the shutdown price.

(a) Unconstrained Q is 26.

(c) A binding cap below monopoly price raises output toward the efficient quantity, as long as price remains above marginal cost.

problem_text: Summit Seltzer faces a per-unit tax of $7. Inverse demand is P = 122 - 2Q and pre-tax marginal cost is $18.
solution:
(a) The tax raises effective MC to 25. MR = 122 - 4Q = 25, so Q = 24.25.

(b) Price is P = 122 - 2(24.25) = $73.50.

(c) Not necessarily. Pass-through depends on demand curvature; with linear demand here, price rises by part of the tax.

problem_text: Vertex Coffee has inverse demand P = 123 - 2Q and marginal cost $19. The firm chooses the monopoly price $71.
solution:
(a) Q = (123 - 71)/2 = 26.

(b) For inverse demand, point elasticity is -P/(bQ) = -71/(2 x 26) = -1.37.

(c) The monopoly markup is higher when absolute elasticity is lower, as summarized by the inverse elasticity rule.

subparts.label: a
subparts.text: Find the quantity associated with that price.

(c) DWL is the triangle between demand and MC from 26 to 52: $676.

problem_text: Vanta Vacuum has identical customers with demand P = 82 - 2q for each customer. Marginal cost is $12. It can charge a per-unit price plus a membership fee.
solution:
(a) Set usage price equal to MC, so p = $12.

(b) Demand gives q = (82 - 12)/2 = 35.

(c) The fee equals consumer surplus at p = MC: 0.5 x (82 - 12) x 35 = $1225.

(c) The high type usually receives information rent so that it self-selects into the premium option.

problem_text: Atlas Droneworks sells the same service in regular and peak periods. Regular price and quantity are $24 and 304; peak price and quantity are $39 and 184. Marginal cost is $8 in both periods.
solution:
(a) Regular contribution is (24 - 8) x 304 = $4864. Peak contribution is (39 - 8) x 184 = $5704.

(b) Peak pricing can reflect higher willingness to pay or tighter capacity in peak periods.

(c) Prices would tend to equalize if demand conditions and capacity scarcity were the same.

problem_text: Echo Forge sells the same service in regular and peak periods. Regular price and quantity are $24 and 329; peak price and quantity are $39 and 209. Marginal cost is $8 in both periods.
solution:
(a) Regular contribution is (24 - 8) x 329 = $5264. Peak contribution is (39 - 8) x 209 = $6479.

(b) Peak pricing can reflect higher willingness to pay or tighter capacity in peak periods.

(c) Prices would tend to equalize if demand conditions and capacity scarcity were the same.

problem_text: LoomLabs has identical customers with demand P = 112 - 2q for each customer. Marginal cost is $10. It can charge a per-unit price plus a membership fee.

(a) Set usage price equal to MC, so p = $10.

(b) Demand gives q = (112 - 10)/2 = 51.

(c) The fee equals consumer surplus at p = MC: 0.5 x (112 - 10) x 51 = $2601.

problem_text: A customer of Cobalt Cloud has wealth 100 and utility u(w)=sqrt(w). With probability 25.0%, a loss of 31 occurs.
solution:
(a) Expected wealth is 100 - 0.25 x 31 = 92.25.

(b) Expected utility is 9.58. The certainty equivalent solves sqrt(CE) = EU, so CE = 91.71.

(c) Risk premium is expected wealth minus CE: 0.54.

problem_text: Crimson Kite offers full insurance to a consumer with wealth 100, utility sqrt(w), loss 30, and loss probability 20.0%.
solution:
(a) Fair premium is expected loss: 0.20 x 30 = $6.

(b) Without insurance EU = 9.67. Full insurance gives certain wealth 100 - premium. Set sqrt(100 - premium) = 9.67, so premium = $6.43.

(c) A risk-averse consumer is willing to pay a risk premium to avoid uncertainty.

(b) A risk-neutral buyer pays at most the expected cost, $52.80.

(c) Risk aversion raises willingness to pay for the warranty above expected cost.

problem_text: Flux Fitness studies an insurance pool with equal numbers of low-, medium-, and high-risk customers. Expected costs are $1040, $2440, and $5040.
solution:
(a) Average cost with everyone is (1040 + 2440 + 5040)/3 = $2840.

(b) If only high-risk customers buy, break-even premium is $5040.

(c) As lower-risk customers leave, average cost rises, which can further raise premiums.

problem_text: Halo Health sells a device worth $1100 if it works and $0 if it fails. The failure probability is 6.0%.
solution:
(a) WTP equals expected value: (1 - 0.06) x $1100 = $1034.

(b) Producer surplus is $1034 - $934 = $100.

(c) Risk aversion lowers WTP below expected value for uninsured downside risk.

problem_text: Meridian Micro offers full insurance to a consumer with wealth 100, utility sqrt(w), loss 35, and loss probability 20.0%.
solution:
(a) Fair premium is expected loss: 0.20 x 35 = $7.

(b) Without insurance EU = 9.61. Full insurance gives certain wealth 100 - premium. Set sqrt(100 - premium) = 9.61, so premium = $7.60.

(c) A risk-averse consumer is willing to pay a risk premium to avoid uncertainty.

solution:

(b) Producer surplus is $1116 - $1016 = $100.

(c) Risk aversion lowers WTP below expected value for uninsured downside risk.

problem_text: Zenith Zips offers full insurance to a consumer with wealth 100, utility sqrt(w), loss 65, and loss probability 20.0%.
solution:
(a) Fair premium is expected loss: 0.20 x 65 = $13.

(b) Without insurance EU = 9.18. Full insurance gives certain wealth 100 - premium. Set sqrt(100 - premium) = 9.18, so premium = $15.67.

(c) A risk-averse consumer is willing to pay a risk premium to avoid uncertainty.

problem_text: Atlas Droneworks is considering selling a warranty. A product repair costs $680 and occurs with probability 14.0%. Buyers are risk-neutral.
solution:
(a) Expected repair cost is 0.14 x $680 = $95.20.

(b) A risk-neutral buyer pays at most the expected cost, $95.20.

(c) Risk aversion raises willingness to pay for the warranty above expected cost.

problem_text: Aurora Scooters studies an insurance pool with equal numbers of low-, medium-, and high-risk customers. Expected costs are $1390, $2790, and $5390.
solution:
(a) Average cost with everyone is (1390 + 2790 + 5390)/3 = $3190.

(b) If only high-risk customers buy, break-even premium is $5390.

(c) As lower-risk customers leave, average cost rises, which can further raise premiums.

problem_text: BluePeak Batteries sells a device worth $1800 if it works and $0 if it fails. The failure probability is 5.0%.
solution:
(a) WTP equals expected value: (1 - 0.05) x $1800 = $1710.

(b) Producer surplus is $1710 - $1610 = $100.

(c) Risk aversion lowers WTP below expected value for uninsured downside risk.

problem_text: Buyers cannot observe whether Echo Forge's marketplace item is high or low quality. High quality is worth $121 and costs $81; low quality is worth $51 and costs $31. The high-quality share is 40.0%.
solution:
(a) Pooled WTP is 0.40 x 121 + 0.60 x 51 = $79.

(b) High quality participates if $79 >= $81, which is false here; low quality participates if $79 >= $31, which is true here. Therefore, only low-quality sellers participate.

(c) If high-quality sellers exit, average quality falls and the pooled WTP falls too.

problem_text: Buyers cannot observe whether Flux Fitness' marketplace item is high or low quality. High quality is worth $122 and costs $82; low quality is worth $52 and costs $32. The high-quality share is 50.0%.
solution:
(a) Pooled WTP is 0.50 x 122 + 0.50 x 52 = $87.

(b) High quality participates if $87 >= $82, which is true here; low quality participates if $87 >= $32, which is true here. Therefore, both high- and low-quality sellers participate.

(c) If high-quality sellers exit, average quality falls and the pooled WTP falls too.

problem_text: High-quality sellers on Halo Health's platform can buy certification costing $15. Without certification, buyers pay the pooled value $88; certified high-quality sellers can charge $123.
solution:
(a) Gross benefit is $123 - $88 = $35.

(b) They certify if $35 >= $15, which is true here.

(c) A credible certificate must be harder or less valuable for low-quality sellers to mimic.

problem_text: Buyers cannot observe whether Meridian Micro's marketplace item is high or low quality. High quality is worth $125 and costs $83; low quality is worth $55 and costs $30. The high-quality share is 50.0%.
solution:
(a) Pooled WTP is 0.50 x 125 + 0.50 x 55 = $90.

(b) High quality participates if $90 >= $83, which is true here; low quality participates if $90 >= $30, which is true here. Therefore, both high- and low-quality sellers participate.

(c) If high-quality sellers exit, average quality falls and the pooled WTP falls too.

solution:
(a) IC: w + 0.75b - 6 >= w + 0.5b, so (0.25)b >= 6.

(b) PC: w + 0.75b - 6 >= 25.

(c) Minimum bonus is b = $24. Then w = $13.

problem_text: Zenith Zips earns $265 from a successful sale. High effort raises success probability from 50.0% to 75.0% and costs the worker $8.
solution:
(a) Expected revenue gain is (0.75 - 0.5) x $265 = $66.25.

(b) Effort is efficient if $66.25 exceeds effort cost $8, which it does here.

(c) The firm still needs an incentive-compatible contract because effort is hidden.

problem_text: BluePeak Batteries and LoomLabs choose whether to adopt a shared standard. If both adopt, payoffs are (71,71). If neither adopts, payoffs are (45,45). If only one adopts, the adopter gets 29 and the non-adopter gets 55.
solution:
(a) Both Adopt and both Do Not Adopt are Nash equilibria: no one wants to deviate unilaterally from either matching outcome.

(b) Yes. Each firm prefers to match the other's action.

(c) Both Adopt is Pareto better because both get 71 instead of 45.

problem_text: Cobalt Cloud first chooses Enter or Stay Out. If it stays out, payoffs are (0,82). If it enters, Meridian Micro chooses Fight or Accommodate. Fight gives (-12,42); Accommodate gives (32,62).
solution:
(a) If entry occurs, Meridian Micro prefers Accommodate because 62 > 42. Anticipating this, Cobalt Cloud enters because 32 > 0.

(b) Yes, entry occurs.

(c) No. Fighting is not credible because accommodation is better after entry.

problem_text: Crimson Kite and Nebula Noodles choose Clean or Pollute. Clean costs each firm 10. If both are clean, each gets 53 before costs. If one pollutes while the other is clean, the polluter gets 59 and the clean firm gets 37 before any clean cost. If both pollute, each gets 41.
solution:
(a) Both clean gives 43 each. Pollute against clean gives 59 to polluter and 27 to cleaner. Both pollute gives 41 each.

(b) Pollute is dominant: it beats Clean whether the other firm is clean or polluting.

(c) Both would be better at Clean/Clean (43,43) than Pollute/Pollute (41,41), but individual incentives lead to pollution.

problem_text: Echo Forge chooses A or B, and Nova Basket chooses Left or Right. Payoffs are: A/Left (34,44), A/Right (24,24), B/Left (39,14), B/Right (29,34).
solution:
(a) If Nova Basket chooses Left, Echo Forge chooses B (39 > 34). If Right, Echo Forge chooses B (29 > 24).

(b) If Echo Forge chooses A, Nova Basket chooses Left (44 > 24). If B, Nova Basket chooses Right (34 > 14).

(c) The only mutual best response is B/Right.

problem_text: Flux Fitness and Polaris Bikes simultaneously choose High Price or Low Price. If both choose High, payoffs are (55,55). If one chooses Low while the other chooses High, the low-price firm gets 66 and the high-price firm gets 39. If both choose Low, payoffs are (47,47).
solution:
(a) Low Price yields 66 instead of 55 if the rival chooses High, and 47 instead of 39 if the rival chooses Low. It is dominant.

(b) By symmetry, Low Price is dominant for the rival too.

(c) The Nash equilibrium is (Low, Low).

problem_text: Cobalt Cloud first chooses Enter or Stay Out. If it stays out, payoffs are (0,122). If it enters, Meridian Micro chooses Fight or Accommodate. Fight gives (-12,82); Accommodate gives (72,102).
solution:
(a) If entry occurs, Meridian Micro prefers Accommodate because 102 > 82. Anticipating this, Cobalt Cloud enters because 72 > 0.

(b) Yes, entry occurs.

(c) No. Fighting is not credible because accommodation is better after entry.

problem_text: Crimson Kite and Nebula Noodles choose Clean or Pollute. Clean costs each firm 10. If both are clean, each gets 93 before costs. If one pollutes while the other is clean, the polluter gets 99 and the clean firm gets 77 before any clean cost. If both pollute, each gets 81.
solution:
(a) Both clean gives 83 each. Pollute against clean gives 99 to polluter and 67 to cleaner. Both pollute gives 81 each.

(b) Pollute is dominant: it beats Clean whether the other firm is clean or polluting.

(c) Both would be better at Clean/Clean (83,83) than Pollute/Pollute (81,81), but individual incentives lead to pollution.

problem_text: Echo Forge chooses A or B, and Nova Basket chooses Left or Right. Payoffs are: A/Left (74,84), A/Right (64,64), B/Left (79,54), B/Right (69,74).
solution:
(a) If Nova Basket chooses Left, Echo Forge chooses B (79 > 74). If Right, Echo Forge chooses B (69 > 64).

(b) If Echo Forge chooses A, Nova Basket chooses Left (84 > 64). If B, Nova Basket chooses Right (74 > 54).

(c) The only mutual best response is B/Right.

problem_text: Flux Fitness and Polaris Bikes simultaneously choose High Price or Low Price. If both choose High, payoffs are (95,95). If one chooses Low while the other chooses High, the low-price firm gets 106 and the high-price firm gets 79. If both choose Low, payoffs are (87,87).
solution:
(a) Low Price yields 106 instead of 95 if the rival chooses High, and 87 instead of 79 if the rival chooses Low. It is dominant.

(b) By symmetry, Low Price is dominant for the rival too.

(c) The Nash equilibrium is (Low, Low).

problem_text: Halo Health and Prism Produce choose whether to adopt a shared standard. If both adopt, payoffs are (116,116). If neither adopts, payoffs are (90,90). If only one adopts, the adopter gets 74 and the non-adopter gets 100.
solution:
(a) Both Adopt and both Do Not Adopt are Nash equilibria: no one wants to deviate unilaterally from either matching outcome.

(b) Yes. Each firm prefers to match the other's action.

(c) Both Adopt is Pareto better because both get 116 instead of 90.

problem_text: Polaris Bikes and a rival consider colluding in a market with P = 103 - Q and marginal cost $23.
solution:
(a) Joint monopoly total output is (80)/2, so each produces 20 and price is $63.

(b) Cournot output per firm is (80)/3 = 26.67, higher than collusive output per firm.

(c) Each firm has an incentive to secretly expand output when the other restricts output.

(a) It serves min(84, 174) = 84 customers.

(b) Profit is (54 - 15) x 84 = $3276.

(c) Capacity limits can soften price competition because a low-price firm may not be able to serve everyone.

solution_subparts.label: a
solution_subparts.text: It serves min(84, 174) = 84 customers.

solution_subparts.label: c
solution_subparts.text: Capacity limits can soften price competition because a low-price firm may not be able to serve everyone.

subparts.label: a
subparts.text: How many customers can it serve?

subparts.label: b
subparts.text: Compute weekly profit ignoring fixed costs.

subparts.label: c
subparts.text: Why can capacity matter in price competition?

problem_text: Quantum Kettle and a rival compete in quantities. Market demand is P = 125 - Q and both have marginal cost $24.
solution:
(a) BR is qi = (101 - qj)/2.

(b) Symmetry gives q = 33.67 and price P = $57.67.

(c) Profit per firm is (P - MC)q = $1133.44.

(b) The leader chooses qL = (101)/2 = 50.50; follower produces 25.25.

(c) Price is $50.25.

problem_text: Summit Seltzer and a rival sell identical products and have the same constant marginal cost $26. They choose prices simultaneously and have enough capacity to serve the market.
solution:
(a) Price equals marginal cost: $26.

(b) Economic profit is zero.

(c) Any firm pricing above marginal cost can be undercut slightly by the rival, so competition drives price to MC.

subparts.label: c

problem_text: Vertex Coffee and a rival consider colluding in a market with P = 128 - Q and marginal cost $20.
solution:
(a) Joint monopoly total output is (108)/2, so each produces 27 and price is $74.

(b) Cournot output per firm is (108)/3 = 36, higher than collusive output per firm.

(c) Each firm has an incentive to secretly expand output when the other restricts output.

problem_text: Polaris Bikes produces a good with inverse demand P = 101 - Q, private marginal cost $21, and external marginal damage $11 per unit.
solution:
(a) Private equilibrium sets 101 - Q = 21, so Q = 80.

(b) Efficiency sets demand equal to social MC 32, so Q = 69.

(c) The Pigouvian tax equals marginal damage: $11.

solution_subparts.label: a
solution_subparts.text: Private equilibrium sets 101 - Q = 21, so Q = 80.

solution_subparts.label: b
solution_subparts.text: Efficiency sets demand equal to social MC 32, so Q = 69.

solution_subparts.label: c
solution_subparts.text: The Pigouvian tax equals marginal damage: $11.

problem_text: Two departments benefit from a public dashboard built by Prism Produce. Marginal benefits are MB1 = 52 - Q and MB2 = 31 - Q. Marginal cost is $60.
solution:
(a) For a public good, MB1 + MB2 = MC.

(b) (52 - Q) + (31 - Q) = 60, so Q = 11.50.

(c) The same unit is consumed by both users, so social marginal benefit sums their marginal benefits.

solution_subparts.label: a
solution_subparts.text: For a public good, MB1 + MB2 = MC.

solution_subparts.label: c
solution_subparts.text: The same unit is consumed by both users, so social marginal benefit sums their marginal benefits.

subparts.label: c
subparts.text: Why do we add marginal benefits vertically?

problem_text: Each unit of Quantum Kettle's training service gives buyers private marginal benefit $43 and creates external benefit $18 for coworkers. Marginal cost is $30.
solution:
(a) Private net benefit is $13.

(b) Social net benefit is $31.

(c) A per-unit subsidy equal to the external benefit, $18, aligns incentives.

solution_subparts.label: a
solution_subparts.text: Private net benefit is $13.

solution_subparts.label: b
solution_subparts.text: Social net benefit is $31.

solution_subparts.label: c
solution_subparts.text: A per-unit subsidy equal to the external benefit, $18, aligns incentives.

subparts.label: c
subparts.text: What subsidy would align private and social incentives?

problem_text: Sapphire Solar can create a shared data tool used by 12 teams. Each team values access at $11. Total cost is $64.
solution:
(a) Social value is 12 x $11 = $132, so provision is efficient.

(b) If all paid value, revenue would be $132, enough to cover cost.

(c) Each team may hope others pay while it still benefits, causing under-provision.

solution_subparts.label: a
solution_subparts.text: Social value is 12 x $11 = $132, so provision is efficient.

solution_subparts.label: b
solution_subparts.text: If all paid value, revenue would be $132, enough to cover cost.

solution_subparts.label: c
solution_subparts.text: Each team may hope others pay while it still benefits, causing under-provision.

subparts.label: b
subparts.text: Would a private seller charging each team separately cover cost if all paid value?

(a) Social value is 57 x $8 = $456, so provision is efficient.

(b) If all paid value, revenue would be $456, enough to cover cost.

(c) Each team may hope others pay while it still benefits, causing under-provision.

solution_subparts.label: a
solution_subparts.text: Social value is 57 x $8 = $456, so provision is efficient.

solution_subparts.label: b
solution_subparts.text: If all paid value, revenue would be $456, enough to cover cost.

solution_subparts.label: c
solution_subparts.text: Each team may hope others pay while it still benefits, causing under-provision.

subparts.label: b
subparts.text: Would a private seller charging each team separately cover cost if all paid value?

problem_text: Aurora Scooters produces a good with inverse demand P = 150 - Q, private marginal cost $22, and external marginal damage $10 per unit.
solution:
(a) Private equilibrium sets 150 - Q = 22, so Q = 128.

(b) Efficiency sets demand equal to social MC 32, so Q = 118.

(c) The Pigouvian tax equals marginal damage: $10.

solution_subparts.label: a
solution_subparts.text: Private equilibrium sets 150 - Q = 22, so Q = 128.

solution_subparts.label: b
solution_subparts.text: Efficiency sets demand equal to social MC 32, so Q = 118.

solution_subparts.label: c
solution_subparts.text: The Pigouvian tax equals marginal damage: $10.

problem_text: A customer views one Quantum Kettle device and two service hours as perfect complements: U(x,y)=min(x, y/2). Income is $105, Px=$5, Py=$9.
solution:
(a) Perfect complements require y = 2x.

(b) Budget gives 5x + 9(2x) = 105, so x = 4.57 and y = 9.13.

(c) Extra y without matching x does not raise utility.

solution_subparts.label: a
solution_subparts.text: Perfect complements require y = 2x.

solution_subparts.label: b
solution_subparts.text: Budget gives 5x + 9(2x) = 105, so x = 4.57 and y = 9.13.

solution_subparts.label: c
solution_subparts.text: Extra y without matching x does not raise utility.

subparts.label: a
subparts.text: What relation between x and y holds at the optimum?

subparts.label: c
subparts.text: Why not buy extra y beyond that ratio?

problem_text: A consumer sees Sapphire Solar tokens x and transit credits y as perfect substitutes: U=x+y. Income is $98, Px=$8, Py=$5.
solution:
(a) The consumer buys the cheaper utility unit, y.

(b) Quantity is income divided by its price: 19.60.

(c) The consumer is indifferent when Px = Py.

solution_subparts.label: a
solution_subparts.text: The consumer buys the cheaper utility unit, y.

solution_subparts.label: b
solution_subparts.text: Quantity is income divided by its price: 19.60.

solution_subparts.label: c
solution_subparts.text: The consumer is indifferent when Px = Py.

subparts.label: a

subparts.label: c
subparts.text: When would the consumer be indifferent?

problem_text: A consumer has Cobb-Douglas utility over Summit Seltzer units x and good y. Income is $163 and Py=$10. Px rises from $8 to $10.
solution:
(a) With equal shares, x_old = (163/2)/8 = 10.19.

(b) x_new = (163/2)/10 = 8.15.

(c) The higher price makes each unit of x more expensive, so demand for x falls.

solution_subparts.label: a
solution_subparts.text: With equal shares, x_old = (163/2)/8 = 10.19.

solution_subparts.label: c
solution_subparts.text: The higher price makes each unit of x more expensive, so demand for x falls.

subparts.label: a
subparts.text: Find x before the price change.

subparts.label: b
subparts.text: Find x after the price change.

problem_text: A student wants utility level U=104 from U(x,y)=sqrt(xy), where x is Vertex Coffee tutoring hours. Prices are Px=$9 and Py=$10.
solution:
(a) MRS = y/x = Px/Py = 9/10, so x/y = 1.11.

(b) Together with xy = 10816, this gives x = 109.63 and y = 98.66.

(c) Minimum expenditure is $1973.26.

problem_text: A customer views one Meridian Micro device and two service hours as perfect complements: U(x,y)=min(x, y/2). Income is $280, Px=$4, Py=$8.
solution:
(a) Perfect complements require y = 2x.

(b) Budget gives 4x + 8(2x) = 280, so x = 14 and y = 28.

(c) Extra y without matching x does not raise utility.

problem_text: When a rival raises its price from $12 to $14, Echo Forge's weekly sales rise from 1308 to 1470 units.
solution:
(a) Cross-price elasticity is (1470 - 1308)/1308 divided by (14 - 12)/12 = 0.74.

(b) It is positive, so the goods are substitutes.

(c) The firm may gain customers when the rival raises price, so monitoring competitor prices is valuable.

problem_text: Prism Produce observes total cost of $1324 at 153 units and $1740 at 183 units over the relevant range.

(b) Yes, if price exceeds marginal cost, expanding output raises profit locally.

(c) This is an average slope over a range, not necessarily the exact marginal cost at every quantity.

problem_text: When a rival raises its price from $11 to $13, Atlas Droneworks' weekly sales rise from 1368 to 1545 units.
solution:
(a) Cross-price elasticity is (1545 - 1368)/1368 divided by (13 - 11)/11 = 0.71.

(b) It is positive, so the goods are substitutes.

(c) The firm may gain customers when the rival raises price, so monitoring competitor prices is valuable.

problem_text: Vanta Vacuum observes total cost of $1684 at 198 units and $2190 at 228 units over the relevant range.
solution:
(a) Marginal cost is approximately Delta C / Delta q = (2190 - 1684)/(228 - 198) = $16.87.

(b) Yes, if price exceeds marginal cost, expanding output raises profit locally.

(c) This is an average slope over a range, not necessarily the exact marginal cost at every quantity.

problem_text: A student wants utility level U=249 from U(x,y)=sqrt(xy), where x is BluePeak Batteries tutoring hours. Prices are Px=$9 and Py=$11.
solution:
(a) MRS = y/x = Px/Py = 9/11, so x/y = 1.22.

(b) Together with xy = 62001, this gives x = 275.28 and y = 225.23.

(c) Minimum expenditure is $4955.04.

problem_text: Crimson Kite is the only seller in its niche. Inverse demand is P = 270 - 2Q and marginal cost is $19.
solution:
(a) MR = 270 - 4Q. Setting MR = MC gives Q = 62.75 and P = $144.50.

(b) Profit is (P - MC)Q = $7875.12.

(c) Efficient quantity sets P = MC, so monopoly output is lower.
